title: Ensure that the apiserver file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive

input_row.title: Ensure that the apiserver file permissions are set to 644 or more restrictive
input_row.description: Ensure that the apiserver file has permissions of 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.rationale: The apiserver file controls various parameters that set the behavior of the API server. You should restrict its file permissions to maintain the integrity of the file. The file should be writable by only the administrators on the system.
input_row.audit: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the master node. For example, stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/apiserver Verify that the permissions are 644 or more restrictive.
input_row.remediation: Run the below command (based on the file location on your system) on the master node. For example, chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/apiserver Impact: None Default Value: By default, apiserver file has permissions of 644.   89 | P a g e References: 1. https://kubernetes.io/docs/admin/kube-apiserver/
input_row.impact: None Default Value: By default, apiserver file has permissions of 644.   89 | P a g e

step1_initial_assessment:
**AUDIT APPROACH:** Manual

**APPROACH DECISION:**
This control requires verification of the file permissions on the apiserver configuration file (e.g., /etc/kubernetes/apiserver) at the OS level. Kubernetes APIs and kubectl do not expose host file system permissions for static configuration files. The audit instructions explicitly require running OS-level commands (e.g., stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/apiserver) on the master node, which necessitates direct host access (SSH or equivalent). Therefore, this check cannot be automated via Kubernetes APIs or kubectl and requires manual verification.

**MANUAL STEPS:**
1. SSH into each Kubernetes master node where the API server is running.
2. Locate the apiserver configuration file (commonly /etc/kubernetes/apiserver, but the path may vary by installation).
3. Run the following command to check the file permissions:
   ```
   stat -c %a /etc/kubernetes/apiserver
   ```
   (Replace the path with the actual location if different.)
4. Verify that the output is 644 or a more restrictive value (e.g., 640, 600).
5. Confirm that only the file owner (typically root or the Kubernetes administrator) has write permissions, and that group/others do not have write access.
6. If the permissions are more permissive than 644, remediate by running:
   ```
   chmod 644 /etc/kubernetes/apiserver
   ```
7. Repeat for all master nodes in the cluster.

**SOURCE:** CIS KUBERNETES 1.6 BENCHMARK V1.0.0 ARCHIVE
